# Novel Frontline Approaches

## Bispecific Antibodies: From Relapsed/Refractory to Frontline

Bispecific antibodies (BsAbs), which simultaneously engage a tumor-associated antigen (typically CD20) and the CD3 receptor on T cells to redirect cytotoxic T-cell activity against malignant B cells, have emerged as transformative agents in DLBCL. Several BsAbs---including glofitamab, epcoritamab, and mosunetuzumab---have received regulatory approval for relapsed or refractory (R/R) DLBCL and are now being actively investigated in the frontline setting [@Kaplon2019Antibodies; @Kaplon2020Antibodies].

Glofitamab, a 2:1 CD20xCD3 bispecific antibody with bivalent binding to CD20, has demonstrated remarkable single-agent activity in heavily pretreated R/R DLBCL, with complete response rates exceeding 35% in patients who had failed prior CAR-T therapy. Its fixed-duration treatment course (12 cycles) and off-the-shelf availability represent practical advantages over cellular therapies. Epcoritamab, a subcutaneously administered CD20xCD3 bispecific antibody, has similarly shown high complete response rates in the R/R setting and offers convenience advantages with its subcutaneous delivery route. Mosunetuzumab was the first BsAb approved for relapsed follicular lymphoma and has demonstrated activity across B-cell malignancies. Frontline trials combining these BsAbs with chemotherapy backbones (such as R-CHOP or pola-R-CHP) are currently enrolling, with the goal of deepening responses and potentially eliminating the need for prolonged maintenance. The integration of BsAbs into first-line treatment could fundamentally reshape the therapeutic landscape, particularly for patients with high-risk disease features who are inadequately served by current chemoimmunotherapy [@SusanibarAdaniya2021].

## CAR-T Cell Therapy: Moving Earlier in the Treatment Paradigm

Chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy, initially approved for third-line and subsequent treatment of R/R large B-cell lymphoma, has progressively moved to earlier lines of therapy. The ZUMA-12 phase II trial evaluated axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) as part of first-line treatment in 40 patients with high-risk large B-cell lymphoma [@Neelapu2022Axicabtagene]. The primary endpoint was met, with a complete response rate of 78% (95% CI, 62--90) and an objective response rate of 89% (95% CI, 75--97) in the 37 efficacy-evaluable patients. At a median follow-up of 15.9 months, 73% of patients remained in objective response, and median duration of response, event-free survival, and PFS were not reached [@Neelapu2022Axicabtagene]. Grade 3 or higher cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and neurologic events occurred in 8% and 23% of patients, respectively, with no treatment-related deaths.

The success of second-line CAR-T trials has further supported the rationale for frontline investigation. The TRANSFORM phase III trial demonstrated that lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel) significantly improved event-free survival compared with standard salvage chemotherapy followed by ASCT as second-line treatment for primary refractory or early relapsed LBCL (median EFS, 10.1 months vs. 2.3 months; P < .001) [@Kamdar2022Lisocabtagene]. The ZUMA-23 trial is a pivotal phase III study evaluating axi-cel versus standard chemoimmunotherapy as first-line treatment in high-risk LBCL, and results are eagerly anticipated. Similarly, the BELINDA trial (tisagenlecleucel vs. standard of care in the second-line setting) did not meet its primary endpoint, highlighting the complexity of optimal CAR-T deployment and the importance of trial design, bridging therapy, and patient selection [@Bishop2022SecondLine; @Amini2022Preparing]. Real-world data from registry analyses have confirmed the efficacy and safety of CAR-T therapies outside clinical trial settings, with the GLA/DRST German registry reporting outcomes consistent with pivotal studies [@Bethge2022GLADRST].

## Targeted Agents by Molecular Subtype

The identification of actionable molecular subtypes has catalyzed efforts to develop subtype-directed frontline strategies. BTK inhibitors represent the most extensively studied class in this context. The Phoenix phase III trial randomized 838 patients with non-GCB DLBCL to ibrutinib (560 mg/day) plus R-CHOP versus placebo plus R-CHOP [@Younes2019Randomized]. Although the study did not meet its primary endpoint of improved event-free survival in the intent-to-treat (HR, 0.934) or ABC populations (HR, 0.949), a preplanned age-stratified analysis revealed a significant treatment-by-age interaction. In patients aged 60 years or younger, ibrutinib plus R-CHOP improved EFS (HR, 0.579), PFS (HR, 0.556), and OS (HR, 0.330), whereas in patients older than 60 years, increased toxicity compromised R-CHOP delivery and worsened outcomes [@Younes2019Randomized]. A subsequent genetic subtype analysis demonstrated that the benefit was concentrated in the MCD and N1 subtypes, with 3-year EFS of 100% for younger patients with these subtypes receiving ibrutinib plus R-CHOP compared with 42.9% and 50% for R-CHOP alone [@Wilson2021Effect].

EZH2 inhibitors, exemplified by tazemetostat, target the EZB subtype characterized by EZH2 gain-of-function mutations and BCL2 translocations. While tazemetostat is approved for relapsed/refractory follicular lymphoma with EZH2 mutations, frontline trials combining EZH2 inhibition with chemoimmunotherapy in EZB-DLBCL are in early development. The BTK signaling pathway also plays a role in primary CNS lymphoma, with ibrutinib monotherapy showing activity in relapsed/refractory disease [@Soussain2019Ibrutinib; @Pal2018Role].

## Lenalidomide Plus R-CHOP (R2-CHOP)

The immunomodulatory agent lenalidomide was hypothesized to preferentially benefit patients with ABC-DLBCL based on phase II data suggesting improved outcomes when combined with R-CHOP. The ROBUST phase III trial tested this hypothesis prospectively, randomizing 570 patients with centrally confirmed ABC-DLBCL to lenalidomide (15 mg/day, days 1--14) plus R-CHOP versus placebo plus R-CHOP for six cycles [@Nowakowski2021ROBUST]. Despite being the first DLBCL phase III study to integrate biomarker-driven patient selection, ROBUST did not meet its primary endpoint of improved PFS. The most common grade 3/4 adverse event was neutropenia, occurring in 60% of the R2-CHOP arm, and treatment completion rates were lower in the lenalidomide arm (74% vs. 84%) [@Nowakowski2021ROBUST]. The negative result of ROBUST, combined with the Phoenix trial findings, highlights the challenge of translating biologically rational combinations into clinically meaningful improvements in unselected or broadly defined molecular subgroups.

## Antibody-Drug Conjugates Beyond Polatuzumab

The clinical success of polatuzumab vedotin in the POLARIX trial has invigorated the development of additional antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) for DLBCL [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab]. ADCs combine the target specificity of monoclonal antibodies with the cytotoxic potency of small-molecule payloads, delivering chemotherapy directly to antigen-expressing tumor cells while limiting systemic exposure [@Chau2019Antibodydrug; @Joubert2020Antibodydrug]. The technology has evolved considerably, with advances in linker chemistry, payload selection, and antibody engineering improving therapeutic indices [@Deeks2019Polatuzumab].

Novel ADC targets under investigation in B-cell lymphomas include CD19, CD22, and ROR1. Loncastuximab tesirine, a CD19-directed ADC carrying a pyrrolobenzodiazepine dimer payload, is approved for R/R DLBCL after two or more prior lines of therapy and is being evaluated in earlier treatment settings. The optimization of ADC design continues to address challenges including on-target off-tumor toxicity, payload resistance, and antigen heterogeneity [@Nguyen2023Mechanisms]. Combination strategies pairing ADCs with checkpoint inhibitors, bispecific antibodies, or targeted agents represent an active area of investigation, with the potential to create synergistic anti-tumor activity while managing overlapping toxicities [@Sehn2020Polatuzumab]. The convergence of multiple novel drug classes---BsAbs, CAR-T cells, ADCs, and molecularly targeted agents---into the frontline treatment paradigm promises to fundamentally transform the management of DLBCL in the coming decade.
